coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic activity enhancement of PDI supermolecular via π-π action and energy level adjusting with graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jun
Miao, Hong
Jing, Jianfang
Zhu, Yongfa
Choi, Wonyong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, perylene bisimide supramolecular (PDI) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were successfully layer-by-layer assembled via electrostatic interaction. The apparent rate constant of photocatalytic reaction for phenol degradation of GQDs/PDI-14 % (0.018 min−1) was 4.73 times as high as nano PDI under visible light. Besides, the rate of H2 production of GQDs/PDI-14 % (1.6 mmol g−1· h−1) was 1.88 times over that of pure PDI. GQDs is helpful for the electrons delocalization via π-π action with PDI. Moreover, the quantum confinement effect of GQDs promotes electron transfer from GQDs to PDI, which helps the conduction band of PDI shift to more negative position and further enhance the reduction ability of PDI. The work may afford some interesting ideas for designing efficient quantum dots-modified supramolecular organic photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
GQDs
PDI
π-π stacking
Charge separation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic degradation activity and H2 production performance of PDI enhanced by GQDs
High separation efficiency of photogenic carriers enhanced by PDI π-π stacking structure-induced via GQDs
Enhanced mechanism for photocatalytic activity of PDI by GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The apparent rate constant k of samples for phenol degradation under visible light (λ > 420 nm); (B) HPLC spectrum of phenol in the presence of GQDs/PDI-14 %; (C) The visible-light phenol degradation plot of samples including nano PDI, GQDs/PDI, commercial PDI, g-C3N4 and Bi2WO6; (D) Recycling tests of GQDs/PDI-14 % for phenol degradation at pH = 2.

(A) Time-dependent hydrogen evolution over pure PDI and GQDs/PDI-14 % under visible light irradiation (687 mW/cm2); (B) Cycling runs for the hydrogen evolution by GQDs/PDI under visible light irradiation.
(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of PDI monomer in DMF solution, nano PDI and GQDs/PDI suspension in water (pH = 2); (B) Raman spectra of PDI and GQDs/PDI-.14 %.

(A) Photocurrents of PDI and GQDs/PDI-14 % under visible light (Cut-off filter 420 nm); (B) EIS plots of PDI and GQDs/PDI-14 %; (C) The fluorescence emission spectrum (PL) of photocatalysts; (D) The SPV spectrum of PDI and GQDs/PDI-14 %.

(A) Free radical capture experiment of GQDs/PDI-14 % for phenol degradation under visible light; EPR spectra of GQDs/PDI-14 % composite for detection of (B) O2
−, (C) PDI
− and (D)
OH under visible light.
The molecular orbitals of GQDs/PDI and energy level diagram of PDI and GQDs/PDI (The results was calculated by DFT, B3LYP/6-311 G).
The separation process of photo-generated carriers and photocatalytic process.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic activity enhancement of PDI supermolecular via π-π action and energy level adjusting with graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Environmental Science and Engineering, Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), Pohang 37673, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Environmental Science and Engineering
Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Environmental Science and Engineering, Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), Pohang 37673, Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The separation process of photo-generated carriers and photocatalytic process.

Herein, perylene bisimide supramolecular (PDI) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were successfully layer-by-layer assembled via electrostatic interaction. The apparent rate constant of photocatalytic reaction for phenol degradation of GQDs/PDI-14 % (0.018 min−1) was 4.73 times as high as nano PDI under visible light. Besides, the rate of H2 production of GQDs/PDI-14 % (1.6 mmol g−1· h−1) was 1.88 times over that of pure PDI. GQDs is helpful for the electrons delocalization via π-π action with PDI. Moreover, the quantum confinement effect of GQDs promotes electron transfer from GQDs to PDI, which helps the conduction band of PDI shift to more negative position and further enhance the reduction ability of PDI. The work may afford some interesting ideas for designing efficient quantum dots-modified supramolecular organic photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Today in the 21 st century, energy crisis and environmental pollution have become the two most serious problems for human beings. Semiconductor photocatalysis as its environmental friendliness, high efficiency, and cost-effective has been extensively researched for solving the two major problems [1]. Among all kinds of photocatalysts, metal-containing photocatalysts have been widely studied, such as titanium dioxide [2], zinc oxide [3], tungsten trioxide [4], bismuth-based semiconductors [5], etc. Despite considerable efforts have been made to develop metal catalysts, many challenges still remain, such as their vulnerability, high price and poor durability. The worst is their negative impact on the environment, which is against with the goal of the consecutive development. As a result, attention of researchers has shifted dramatically to reducing the dependence on metals and cutting the cost of catalysts [6].

Unlike metal-containing semiconductor materials, metal-free photocatalysts have been widely studied because of their cost-effectiveness and superior sustainability. They only include non-metal elements, such as carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, boron, phosphorus, which are very abundant on earth. At present, metal-free photocatalysts mainly include C3N4 [7,8], black phosphorus [9,10], COF materials [11,12], and supramolecular organic materials [13,14]. Self-assembled supramolecular materials, such as porphyrin [15], phthalocyanine [16], perylene imide (PDI) [17], have been emerging in photocatalysis. In particular, the typical n-type organic semiconductors, PDI-based materials, have many superiorities, such as excellent charge mobility, stability and electron affinity [18–20]. They have been used in photocatalytic oxygen production [17,21], photocatalytic degradation [22] and selective organic reaction [23]. However, PDI-based materials still have the disadvantage of easy recombination of photo-induced carriers, which leads to their low photocatalytic performance. At present, modification methods for PDI mainly include forming π-π interactions [24], introducing electron donor and acceptor units [25], and changing functional groups [26]. These methods can improve the carrier separation in different degree via increasing the built-in electric field, but the synthesis methods are complex and take a long time. In our previous work, Ag2S/PDI heterojunction has been successfully synthesized by two-step electrostatic interaction [27]. However, considering the high cost of silver sulfide, we tried to modify PDI by selecting some materials that are cheap and easy to obtain.

Carbon, as the most common and necessary element, plays a very important role in people's life and industry. Some allotropic materials of carbon have been found, such as C60 discovered in 1985 [28], 1D carbon nanotubes in 1991 [29], 2D graphene in 2004 [30]. They all make a critical difference during the development of advanced materials due to their excellent mechanical, thermal and electronic properties [31,32]. They are widely used in various fields, such as photovoltaic cells [33], supercapacitors [34] and environmental remediation [35]. GQDs, as a unique zero-dimensional (0D) materials, has been extensively researched on account of its excellent electronic and physicochemical properties [36,37]. Their framework is similar to graphene. However, compared to 2D graphene, zero-dimensional GQDs can be used as highly active reaction sites due to its ultra-small particle size (< 10 nm) [38]. It has been widely used in photocatalysis due to its excellent conductivity, nontoxicity and quantum confinement effect [39,40]. Graphene quantum dots can be used as both electron donor and electron acceptor, which can well regulate the transfer of photogenerated charge.

Herein, graphene quantum dots are selected to modify the self-assembled PDI. They are combined together via electrostatic interaction. The structure, photocatalytic properties, stability and photocatalytic mechanism are systematically studied. The GQDs/PDI composite has higher visible-light photocatalytic degradation property than pure nano PDI. Additionally, it should be pointed out that the H2 production performance of PDI and GQDs/PDI is also investigated under visible light, which is seldom reported. Satisfyingly, the H2 production property of nano PDI has been improved after modification by GQDs. In a word, the study may provide some new ideas for the construction of metal-free systems with supramolecular organic photocatalysts.
The bulk PDI was prepared according to the reported literature [26] and GQDs were prepared by an electrolytic method [45]. The synthetic method was elaborated in the supporting document.

Firstly, a certain quality of bulk PDI (0.5432 g) was added in 200 mL deionized water. Subsequently, 800 μL triethylamine was added and then stirred for thirty minutes. Then 35 mL HCl (4.0 M) was added and continued stirring for three hours. The self-assembly PDI nanofibers would be formed. It was washed thoroughly with distilled water until the pH of filtrate became neutral. Then the collected solid was dried at 70 °C. The sample was labeled as nano PDI.

GQDs/PDI composite with different mass ratio were synthesized by electrostatic interaction. Firstly, 0.025 g nano PDI was fully dispersed in 100 mL deionized water. Then, different volumes of graphene quantum dot solutions were added (0.5 mg/mL, pH = 1). The solution volumes of graphene quantum dots were 1 mL, 3 mL, 5 mL, 7 mL, 9 mL and 11 mL, respectively. That is, the mass of graphene quantum dots accounts for 2%, 6%, 10 %, 14 %, 18 % and 22 % of PDI, respectively. The mixture was stirred for 1 h, and then ultrasound at high power for 3 h. Finally, it was filtered through 0.22 μm filter membranes, and the obtained red solids were dried at 70 °C in an oven.

The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) results were recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer. The working current and potential were respectively 40 mA and 40 kV. The TEM was used to observe the morphology of the materials on the Hitachi HT 7700 electron microscope. GQDs, nano PDI and GQDs/PDI composite were dispersed in deionized water via ultrasonic treatment and then dropped onto the copper mesh. HRTEM was conducted on the JEM 2010 F electron microscope and the accelerating voltage was 200 kV. The Hitachi U-3010 spectrophotometer was used to detect DRS spectra and BaSO4 was selected as the reference. The solid samples were measured by integrating sphere and the scanning range is from 200 nm to 900 nm. The FT-IR spectra could be obtained by a Bruker VERTEX 700 spectrometer. Raman spectra were performed on the HORIBA HR800 spectrometer and the laser wavelength was at 514 nm. The fluorescence spectra were obtained on the Edinburgh FS5 spectrophotometer and the excitation wavelength was at 612 nm. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on the PHI-Quanter spectrometer. ζ potential was obtained by Horiba SZ-100 Nano Particle analyzer. The ESR was carried out on the JES-FA200 paramagnetic resonance spectrometer. DMPO-methanol, DMPO-H2O and TEMPO-H2O were used as trapping agents to detect superoxide radical, hydroxyl radical and singlet oxygen, respectively. The steady-state surface photovoltage (SPV) measurement system includes computer, lock-in amplifier, monochromatic light and photovoltaic cell. The monochromatic light was provided by xenon lamp (500 W) and a double-prism monochromator. The construction of the photovoltaic cell structure is ITO-sample-ITO.
Photoelectrochemical measurements were elaborated in the supporting information.

The visible-light (500 W xenon lamp, 420 nm cut-off filter) photocatalytic degradation property was evaluated by degrading phenol (5 ppm). Specifically speaking, 25 mg catalyst and 50 mL of phenol solution was firstly added in silica tube. The pH of solution was adjusted by 0.1 M HCl. After ultrasonic treatment, the solution was stirred for 1 h under dark to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then quartz tube was irradiated by visible light. After that, 2.5 ml of reaction mixture was taken every 20 min. The phenol concentration was detected via the high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC).
The photocatalytic hydrogen production experiment was elaborated in the supplementary document.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation activity and H2 production performance of PDI enhanced by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidation and reduction capacity of samples was firstly evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of phenol and hydrogen production under visible light (λ > 420 nm). Satisfyingly, GQDs/PDI exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance. Specifically, as shown in Fig. 1 A, GQDs/PDI showed the best photocatalytic performance when the mass fraction of graphene quantum dots was 14 % of PDI. The degradation rate of GQDs/PDI-14 % (0.01831 min−1) was 4.73 times that of pure PDI (0.00387 min−1). The degradation curve over time and the corresponding kinetics curve ﬁtting were shown in Fig. S1. The result shows that the photocatalytic degradation reaction accorded with the first order reaction kinetics. What's more, GQDs/PDI has a better photodegradation activity than some reported visible-light photocatalysts (Fig. 1C), such as Bi2WO6, commercial self-assembly PDI and g-C3N4. The intermediates were detected by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Fig. 1B). Obviously, the peak of phenol located at 3.512 min gradually decreased with irradiation. Besides, the peak of 2.487 min and 1.319 min respectively match with p-benzoquinone and maleic acid [27]. However, the peak of 0.507 min may belong to the small PDI molecule dissolution. Fig. 1D shows the photodegradation stability of GQDs/PDI-14 % in acid environment (pH = 2). It can be seen that GQDs/PDI composite shows high photocatalytic stability.

In addition, as shown in Fig. 2 A, the hydrogen production rate of GQDs/PDI-14 % (1.60 mmol g−1· h−1) was about 1.88 times as high as pure nanofiber PDI (0.85 mmol g−1· h−1). Besides, the stability of hydrogen production of GQDs/PDI-14 % was investigated. It is surprising that the hydrogen production stability of the sample was very good and maintained a relatively high H2 production rate (Fig. 2B). Besides, Fig. S2 showed the gas chromatogram of H2 production of PDI and GQDs/PDI. Obviously, the amount of H2 produced by GQDs/PDI composite increased gradually with the extension of illumination time and the H2 peak area of GQDs/PDI was higher than that of pure PDI at the same reaction time. Furthermore, the AQY at different monochromatic light was also measured via different band-pass filters. As shown in Fig. S3, the AQY of GQDs/PDI-14 % was higher than that of pure nanofiber PDI at different wavelength. The apparent quantum yield (AQY) of GQDs/PDI (0.5 %) is 1.85 times as high as pure PDI (0.27 %) at 420 nm. Especially, the AQY of GQDs/PDI is 1.04 % at 500 nm, nearly 2.48 times higher than that of individual PDI (0.42 %, 500 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: High separation efficiency of photogenic carriers enhanced by PDI π-π stacking structure-induced via GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The thought-provoking question is why GQDs/PDI exhibits higher photocatalytic performance than pure PDI? Firstly, the structure of the samples was explored in detail. As for PDI, the stacking mode of PDI molecules with rigid π-electron plane mainly includes H-type and J-type aggregates [41,42]. According to previous research, H-type PDI contributes to the generation superoxide radicals (·O2 −) and holes (h+) through electron-transfer [42]. The π-π stacking type can be obtained by UV–vis absorption spectra as shown in Fig. 3 A. The spectrum of monomer PDI dissolved in DMF shows three pronounced peaks at 458 nm, 489 nm and 524 nm, which are caused by the electronic transitions of monomeric PDI molecules [43]. However, as for self-assembly nanofiber PDI, the maximum absorption peak position shows a significant blue-shift of about 52 nm and the fine structure of spectral peak is lost, demonstrating the stacking mode of PDI molecules is H-type [44]. The interesting thing is that the absorption spectrum of GQDs/PDI even shows a greater blue-shift of 63 nm compared with the PDI monomer, which indicates that GQDs do not affect the H-type π-π stacking mode of nanofiber PDI. On the contrary, GQDs are beneficial to a higher π-π stacking degree of nano PDI. Highly ordered stacking helps to form the long-range electrons delocalization and contributes to the migration of photoelectrons.

The improved π-π stacking degree can be further demonstrated by Raman. Fig. 3B shows the Raman spectra of pure PDI and GQDs/PDI. As for PDI, the peaks at about 1585 cm−1 and 1289 cm−1 are respectively assigned to the C = C and C–H. The π–π stacking degree can be evaluated by the peak intensity ratio value of 1585 cm−1/1289 cm−1 [26]. Test results show that the peak intensity ratio value of GQDs/PDI (0.84) is higher than that of pure PDI (0.68), indicating GQDs promote the ordered π-π stacking degree of PDI, which is helpful for the long-range electrons delocalization and the transfer of photoelectrons. XRD (Fig.S4A) results also can reflect the π-π accumulation degree of PDI, which was elaborated in the supporting information.

Think more carefully, what makes the π-π stacking degree of GQD/PDI increase? Here, the forming process of GQDs/PDI has to be considered. The preparation of GQDs/PDI, morphology analysis and zeta potential are respectively illustrated in Figs. S5–S8. According to the analysis in supporting information, it can be concluded that GQDs/PDI is successfully constructed via the electrostatic interaction. Simultaneously, the forming process of GQDs/PDI indicates that the electrostatic interaction between GQDs and PDI maybe the main driving force for enhancing the π-π stacking density of nano PDI.

The separation efficiency of photogenic carriers plays crucial role in the improvement of photocatalytic performance. Theoretically speaking, higher π-π stacking degree helps to form the long- range electrons delocalization and contributes to the migration of photoelectrons, thus further improves the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. So a series of photoelectric tests are used to study the photogenerated carriers behavior. As shown in Fig. 4 A, GQDs/PDI-14 % shows a higher photocurrent response signal than pure nanofiber PDI under visible light, indicating that nanofiber PDI modified with GQDs can effectively facilitate the electron transfer. Furthermore, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) result is displayed in Fig. 4B. The shorter the arc diameter, the smaller the charge transfer resistance [46]. After illumination, both PDI and GQDs/PDI-14 % present shorter arc diameters than dark conditions, demonstrating that under visible light both GQDs/PDI-14 % and individual PDI could be excited. Simultaneously, it can be clearly seen that the arc radius of GQDs/PDI-14 % is smaller than that of PDI, indicating that PDI modified by graphene quantum dots can effectively reduce the resistance of charge transfer. The significantly different EIS result of individual PDI and GQDs/PDI indicates that there is a close interaction between GQDs and PDI, which is helpful to the charge migration.

In addition, electron-hole pair recombination can be reflected by fluorescence emission. Low fluorescence emission intensity indicates that electron/hole pairs are less likely to recombine [47]. As for pure PDI, an obvious emission peak signal at 728 nm can be seen (Fig. 4C). But simultaneously, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of PDI modified by graphene quantum dots is significantly reduced, suggesting that the composite GQDs/PDI could effectively facilitate the separation of h+-e− pairs.

Furthermore, the surface photovoltage (SPV) is used to explore the carrier separation process [48], which is shown in Fig. 4D. Stronger SPV signal indicates higher carrier separation efficiency. The result shows that the SPV signal of PDI is mainly distributed between 400 nm and 700 nm, which is caused by electron transition. Obviously, the GQDs/PDI has a stronger SPV response than individual PDI, indicating that GQDs/PDI can more effectively facilitate the separation of photo-induced carriers. Because of the same SPV test conditions, the different SPV signal intensity of PDI and GQDs/PDI can be put down to the interaction between GQDs and PDI. It can be inferred that there is an effective electron transfer process between GQDs and PDI, which facilitates the separation of photo-induced carriers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced mechanism for photocatalytic activity of PDI by GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic mechanism was investigated by three aspects, namely, the active species involved in the reaction, adaptive level oriented electron transfer from GQDs to PDI, photocatalytic oxidation and reduction process with thermodynamical analysis. During photocatalytic reactions, many active species are involved, such as superoxide radical (O2
−), hole (h+), hydroxyl radical (
OH), and PDI radical anions (PDI
−). To explore the main active species, the free radical trapping tests and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) were conducted as shown Fig. 5 . As shown in Fig. 5A, different capture agents were used in the photocatalytic degradation, including t-BuOH (
OH scavenger) and formic acid (h+ scavenger). In addition, nitrogen was injected into the reaction solution to explore the role of superoxide radicals. Obviously, the catalytic property is decreased after adding HCOOH, suggesting h+ is the pivotal active species. Moreover, the SPV signal is positive as shown in Fig. 4D, which also proves that h+ is indeed involved in photocatalytic reactions [15]. Additionally, the photocatalytic property reduced when nitrogen was injected into the reaction solution, demonstrating O2
− is also involved in the reaction. Obviously,
OH shows a weak effect on the reaction. Furthermore, O2
− is also be confirmed by EPR test. As for GQDs/PDI-14 % sample, the O2
− signal could not be found without light (0 min). When the light is added, there is an obvious superoxide radical signal (Fig. 5B). Besides, as shown in Fig.5C and Fig. S18, both PDI and GQDs/PDI could produce PDI radical anion (PDI
−). Moreover, the signals of PDI and GQDs/PDI enhanced over time under the irradiation and then slowly reduced when the light was shut off. Especially, GQDs/PDI could produce more PDI radical anion (PDI
−), which can be proved via the selected integral EPR spectra of PDI and GQDs/PDI after irradiation for 20 min (Fig. S19). It indicated that photogenerated electrons of GQDs could flowed into PDI to produce more PDI radical anion, which was conducive to photocatalytic performance. The phenomenon of time-dependent decrease of EPR bands under irradiation were not found, which indicated that there is not the back electron transfer of the composite material. The EPR result of
OH is shown in Fig. 5D and the weak
OH signal demonstrates that hydroxyl radical is not the primary active species. The result matches with the free radical trapping test.

In addition, the band structure of catalysts is closely associated with the photocatalytic redox reaction. The band gap of PDI is confirmed to be 1.75 eV as shown in Fig. S4B. Furthermore, the semiconductor type of PDI is determined by Mott − Schottky test (Fig. S14). The result shows that the slope of PDI is positive, demonstrating that nano PDI is a n-type semiconductor [17,26]. Simultaneously, it can be seen that the flat band potential of individual PDI is -0.24 eV vs. NHE. On the basis of the reported literature, the conduction band position of n- type semiconductor is deeper than the flat band potential (0.1 − 0.2 eV) [17]. Here, the difference value is set to be 0.1 eV, then the conduction band bottom of PDI is determined to be −0.34 eV vs. NHE. Therefore, according to the equation “EVB=ECB + Eg”, the valence band edge of PDI can be calculated (1.41 eV). In the same way, the band position of GQDs/PDI can be obtained, the conduction band and the valence band are respectively -0.39 eV vs. NHE and 1.32 eV vs. NHE. The results showed that the band gap of PDI modified by GQDs became narrower, and the CB and VB positions moved to a more negative direction.

To illuminate the effect of GQDs in the system, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum were used to elucidate the electron transition of GQDs (Fig. S15). Based on the analysis in supporting information, it can be concluded that there are electronic transition processes from HOMO (the σ, π and n orbitals in highest occupied molecular orbitals) to LUMO (the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals) in GQDs.

Furthermore, the frontier orbitals of GQDs and PDI molecule were shown in Fig. S17. Obviously, the HOMO and LUMO levels of PDI molecule contain carbon and oxygen elements only from perylene core, and the HOMO and LUMO of GQDs are distributed on the graphite carbon. The LUMO/HOMO levels of PDI monomer were calculated to be −4.0345/−6.5178 eV, which is lower than GQDs (−2.2493/−5.6134 eV). Besides, the frontier orbitals of GQDs/PDI were obtained as shown in Fig. 6 . As for GQDs/PDI, it was found that the HOMO orbital was mainly located on the GQDs and the LUMO orbital was located on PDI. The results indicated the existence of effective charge-transfer transition from GQDs to PDI. The HOMO orbital energy of GQDs/PDI was -5.6182 eV and the LUMO is -3.9901 eV. The calculated HOMO-LUMO gap of GQDs/PDI was 1.6281 eV, which is narrower than that of PDI (2.4833 eV). The result was consistent with the change trend of energy band structure corresponding to Table S2 obtained via experimental test.

Combined with the theoretical calculation, the photocatalytic mechanism was proposed as shown in Fig. 7 . Specifically, when the sample GQDs/PDI was irradiated, the electrons in the HOMO orbital of GQDs can transfer to the LUMO orbital of PDI monomer, which forms an effective charge transfer within the molecule. The effective charge transfer can be reflected in the photoelectric test (Fig. 4). Thereby the photogenerated electrons in conduction band of nano-PDI will increase. The unique π-π stacking structure of PDI provides a fast transmission channel for the photogenerated electrons, so that the photogenerated electrons can more easily reach the catalyst surface. So how do photo-induced electrons take part in photocatalytic reactions? On the one side, in the photocatalytic degradation, the dissolved oxygen in water will combine the photo-induced electrons on the surface of GQDs to form superoxide radical. On the other side, due to the effective electron transfer from GQDs to PDI, electrons accumulated on the surface of PDI can reduce H+ in water to H2. Simultaneously, more PDI radical anion will be formed in GQDs/PDI, which is conducive to hydrogen production. Platinum as the co-catalyst also accelerates the H2 production rate.

Thermodynamically, the GQDs/PDI has more negative CB position (-0.39 eV, vs. NHE) compared with that of Eθ (O2/O2
−) (-0.33 eV, vs. NHE), suggesting that O2
− can be formed on the CB of GQDs/PDI [49]. And it conforms to the active species capture test and EPR results. O2
− is a kind of high active oxidants, which can decompose organic pollutants. Similarly, how do holes participate in photocatalytic reactions? Although the VB position of GQDs/PDI has moved in the more positive direction, the holes on the VB still possess certain oxidation capacity according to the captured experimental results. In photocatalytic degradation, the h+ can directly decompose organic pollutants. In the hydrogen production reaction, the sacrificial agent (ascorbic acid) can be oxidized by holes to reduce the recombination of h+ and e- and to promote the efficiency of hydrogen production.

In summary, PDI supramolecular and GQDs were successfully layer-by-layer assembled via electrostatic interaction. The metal-free GQDs/PDI showed enhanced photocatalytic activity than pure PDI. GQDs were beneficial to the formation of long-range electrons delocalization via π-π action with PDI. In addition, the quantum confinement effect of GQDs promotes electron transfer from GQDs to PDI, which helps the conduction band of PDI shift to more negative position and further enhance the reduction ability of PDI. In short, this work may afford some interesting ideas for constructing high-performance quantum dots-modified supramolecular organic photocatalysts.

Jun Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Hong Miao: Investigation. Jianfang Jing: Investigation. Yongfa Zhu: Methodology, Supervision, Writing - review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Wonyong Choi: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by Chinese National Science Foundation (21673126, 21761142017, 21621003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2020.119547.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen producing water treatment through solar photocatalysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Kim, W. Choi, Hydrogen producing water treatment through solar photocatalysis, Energy Environ. Sci. 3 (2010) 1042-1045.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoinduced charge transfer processes in solar photocatalysis based on modified TiO2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Park, H.-i Kim, G.-h. Moon, W. Choi, Photoinduced charge transfer processes in solar photocatalysis based on modified TiO2, Energy Environ. Sci. 9 (2016) 411-433.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of the TiO2-ZnO heterostructure on the photoinduced hydrophilic conversion of TiO2 and ZnO surfaces

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.V. Rudakova, A.V. Emeline, D.W. Bahnemann, Effect of the TiO2-ZnO Heterostructure on the Photoinduced Hydrophilic Conversion of TiO2 and ZnO Surfaces, J. Phys. Chem. C 123 (2019) 8884-8891.
